title: Electrically controlled Heat Assisted Magnetic Recording in Intercalated 2D Magnets
authors: Josue Rodriguez, Ruishi Qi, Catherine Xu, Feng Wang, James G. Analytis, Hossein Taghinejad
abstract: The ever-increasing demand for fast, reliable, and energy-efficient information storage continues to push magnetic memory technologies toward their fundamental limits. Conventional scaling strategies, which rely on reducing bit size, inevitably run into the "magnetic recording trilemma," where signal-to-noise ratio, thermal stability, and writability cannot all be optimized simultaneously. Heat-assisted magnetic recording (HAMR) has emerged as the leading solution, enabling high-density storage by transiently heating the medium during the write cycle. However, the reliance on laser optics and plasmonic transducers restricts HAMR primarily to hard-disk drives, limiting its integration with on-chip or embedded architectures. Here, we demonstrate an electronic variant of HAMR in which Joule heating from low-current density current pulses facilitates data writing, while the anomalous Hall effect provides electronic readout. Employing intercalated 2D magnet Ni$_{1/4}$TaSe$_2$, we show direct evidence that current pulses heat the material above its Curie temperature, during which a small magnetic field of ~2mT (100 times smaller than the coercive field) enables efficient data writing. The all-electronic approach combined with the 2D magnetic medium creates timely opportunities to revisit the energy-assisted magnetization recording, enabling new recording schemes that combine fundamental novelty with technological impact.
summary: This paper presents a method for all-electronic heat-assisted magnetic recording using the 2D magnet Ni1/4TaSe2. By using Joule heating from electrical pulses to transiently heat the material above its Curie temperature, the authors can switch magnetic states using much smaller magnetic fields than usual. This approach offers a path toward highly scalable, energy-efficient, and integrated magnetic memory for next-generation computing.
main_problem: The magnetic recording trilemma makes it difficult to scale high-density storage without sacrificing thermal stability or writability. Existing Heat-Assisted Magnetic Recording (HAMR) relies on bulky laser optics, which are hard to integrate into on-chip architectures.
main_result: The authors demonstrate an all-electronic variant of HAMR using Joule heating in the 2D magnet Ni1/4TaSe2, allowing magnetization reversal with a magnetic field 100 times smaller than the coercive field.
method: The researchers used transport measurements (Anomalous Hall Effect) and MOKE imaging on Hall-bar devices patterned from Ni1/4TaSe2 crystals via focused-ion beam milling to observe magnetization switching induced by current pulses.
model_or_system: The physical system is an intercalated 2D itinerant metallic ferromagnet, Ni1/4TaSe2, which features perpendicular magnetic anisotropy and a Curie temperature of approximately 55 K.
key_observables: Anomalous Hall resistivity (rho_xy), longitudinal resistivity (rho_xx), coercive field (Bc), and the shift in apparent Curie temperature (Delta T).
important_parameters: Coercive field of ~0.2 T, writing field of ~2 mT, threshold current density of ~10 x 10^4 A/cm^2, and current pulse duration of 1 ms.
main_assumptions: The demonstration is currently limited to low-temperature regimes (cryogenic temperatures) where the material is ferromagnetic.
figures_summary: Figure 1 introduces the magnetic recording trilemma and the HAMR concept; Figure 2 shows the device SEM image, Hall hysteresis loops, and response to current; Figure 4 illustrates the temperature dependence of resistance and the quadratic scaling of temperature shift with current density.
paper_structure: The paper begins by motivating the need for electronic HAMR, introduces the Ni1/4TaSe2 material and device fabrication, presents transport measurements establishing the magnetic baseline, demonstrates the current-induced switching mechanism, provides evidence for Joule heating via temperature scaling, and concludes with potential applications and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Magnetic recording trilemma and HAMR concept. a The trilemma of magnetic recording: increas- ing areal density by shrinking bit size imposes contradic- tory requirements for simultaneously maintaining reasonable SNR (depends on volume, V), thermal stability (depends on anisotropy, Ku, and V), and writing field (Bw, depends on Ku). Optimizing one metric necessarily compromises another. b HAMR circumvents this limitation: transiently heating the sample above its Curie temperature reduces the anisotropy barrier, enabling bit flipping with modest fields during the write cycle. Upon cooling, the magnetic anisotropy is re- stored to a large value, ensuring thermal stability for long- term...

FIG. 2. Current-induced magnetization reversal in a Ni1/4TaSe2 FIB device. a False colored SEM micrograph of a Ni1/4TaSe2 crystal patterned into a Hall bar. The magnetic field is applied along the out-of-plane direction (i.e., z-direction), which is the magnetic easy axis. Two current sources are used, a small ac component for electronic readout, and a pulsed DC current for magnetization reversal. b The anomalous Hall resistivity, showing distinct low- and high-resistance states, corresponding to the UP and DOWN magnetization states, respectively. c Anomalous Hall resistivity measured after applying current pulses of various amplitudes. The device was initially set to a low (high)...

FIG. 3. Direct imaging of current-induced magnetization reversal using MOKE. a Current-induced reversal probed electrically via anomalous Hall resistivity (ρxy, left axis) and optically via Kerr rotation (MOKE, right axis). Both readout modalities consistently yield a threshold current density of Jth ∼9.7 × 104A.cm−2. b Sequence of Kerr microscopy maps acquired after successive current pulses of increasing amplitudes, corresponding to the data points presented in panel (a). The device is initialized to the UP state under a +3 T magnetic field and then pulsed under a writing field of Bw = −2 mT. Above the critical current density (J > Jth), the MOKE contrast switches sign, directly...

FIG. 4. Direct measurement of Joule heating. a The temperature dependence of the longitudinal resistance ρxx in response to a small measuring ac current of JAC = 20A.cm−2× cos(2πf◦t) superimposed on a large DC current (JDC) of varying amplitudes as indicated on the figure. The total applied current is Jtotal = JAC + JDC, with ρxx calcu- lated from the measurement of the ac component of Vxx at f◦ frequency. b The shift of the apparent Curie temperature, Tc (JDC ̸= 0), relative to the equilibrium Curie temperature, Tc (JDC = 0), is defined as ∆T = Tc (JDC ̸=0) - Tc (JDC = 0). ∆T quadratically scales with JDC, which is the signature of Joule heating.
